subjects.subject: film-photography
subjects.title: Tripods, Cable Releases, and Stability
subjects.lesson:
First principles: sharpness depends on the weakest link in the stability chain. That chain runs from ground to feet to leg locks to center column to head to quick-release plate to camera body to shutter actuation. If any link flexes or vibrates at the moment of exposure, resolution is lost. Mechanical isolation means preventing vibrations from reaching the film plane in the first place.

Why stability matters
Film often has lower ISO and slower shutter speeds, especially with small apertures for depth of field. Any shake at 1/2s, 1s, or longer is fatal to detail. A tripod, combined with a cable release or timer, removes human tremor and isolates the camera from environmental movement.

Tripod anatomy and choices
- Legs: Carbon fiber is stiffer for weight and damps vibration quickly. Aluminum is cheaper, durable, and transmits vibration a bit longer. Prioritize stiffness over maximum height.
- Sections: Fewer, thicker sections are stiffer. Extend the largest sections first; leave the thinnest retracted when possible.
- Height: Choose a tripod that reaches your working eye level without extending the center column. The center column is a monopod on a tripod—convenient but the weakest link.
- Locks: Twist locks are compact and seal well; lever locks are quick and very secure when adjusted. Either works if maintained.
- Feet: Rubber for hard floors; spikes for soil or ice. The goal is a positive, non-slipping contact with the ground.

Heads and plates
- Ball head: Fast, strong, minimal knobs, good for general use. Look for a large ball and smooth friction control.
- 3-way/pan-tilt: Independent axis control, good for precise framing, slower to use.
- Geared head: Micro adjustments for architecture or macro, excellent when weight is acceptable.
- Quick-release: Use an Arca-Swiss compatible clamp and a plate that fits your camera body tightly. A loose plate is a hidden weak link.

Cable releases and self-timers
Most mechanical film cameras have a threaded shutter button for a cable release. This lets you trip the shutter without touching the camera. For cameras without a thread (or if you forgot the release), use the self-timer: 2s if your mirror is already up; 10s if you need more damping time. On SLRs, mirror lock-up (MLU) reduces internal shock. Leaf-shutter rangefinders and TLRs have less internal vibration but still benefit from hands-off triggering.

Setup technique: making the system stiff
- Placement: Put one leg under the lens direction on level ground. On slopes, put a single leg downhill, lengthened first, for a stable base.
- Stance: Widen the legs until the center of gravity of the camera + lens is clearly inside the triangle. On windier days, go lower and wider.
- Column: Keep the center column down. If you must raise it, do so minimally and avoid touching the camera during exposure.
- Hanging weight: Hanging a bag from the hook can help if it touches the ground or is tethered so it cannot swing. A swinging weight amplifies vibration.
- Contact: Press the tripod feet firmly. On soft ground, push spikes in. On wood or bridges, avoid spans that flex; choose structural supports.
- Damping: After composing, gently press down on the camera for a second to settle the system, then let go and trigger by cable or timer.

Wind and crowds
- Wind: Lower profile, shield the camera with your body, stand upwind as a windbreak, and time exposures between gusts. Avoid umbrellas; they are sails.
- Crowds: Keep legs narrow until final placement. Use a high-visibility strap or small light on a leg at night to reduce trip hazards. Be courteous; step aside between exposures. If vibration from foot traffic is a problem, wait for lulls.

Uneven and difficult terrain
- Rocks: Place each foot on a separate, stable surface. Test with a light push before mounting the camera.
- Sand: Use wide feet or a small pad to prevent sinking. Brush sand from locks before collapsing legs.
- Water: Salt water corrodes; rinse aluminum legs after use; keep joints clean.

Exposure timing and internal shock
- Shutter shock: At certain speeds (often 1/2s to 1/15s), internal movements can blur images. MLU plus cable release avoids this. If no MLU, use the self-timer to let the system settle after mirror slap.
- Long exposures: Over several seconds, transient shakes matter less than sustained movement. Ensure the rig is steady for the whole duration.

Testing your setup
- Do a 1-second exposure test with a 50mm lens and fine-grain film. Inspect grain sharpness with a loupe. Repeat with the center column up, then down; with and without cable release. Learn how each choice affects results.

Choosing a kit by weakest-link thinking
- Camera weight + longest lens define head and leg stiffness needs.
- If budget is tight, prioritize a solid head and rigid legs at working height. A great head on wobbly legs (or vice versa) still yields blur. Balance the chain.

Maintenance
- Keep locks clean and snug. Re-tension lever locks as needed. Check plate screws often. Replace worn rubber feet.

The goal is a quiet, inert camera at the moment of exposure. Every decision—gear, placement, triggering—serves that one principle.
subjects.example: You want a 1-second exposure at f/11 of a waterfall with a 50mm SLR. You set your carbon fiber tripod to chest height with the center column down, extend thicker leg sections first, and place one leg pointing toward the falls. On wet rock, you twist on rubber feet for grip. You compose, then enable mirror lock-up, wait a breath, and fire with a cable release. Wind picks up, so you lower the legs a notch, shield the camera with your body, and retry. On the contact sheet, the MLU + cable frame is crisper than the one you took earlier by pressing the shutter directly.
subjects.scenario: At a night market you plan 2–4 second exposures to capture light trails. It’s crowded and windy between stalls. You choose a low, wide stance near a solid wall, keeping legs tucked in until you’re set. You hang your bag from the hook but tether it so it touches the ground and cannot swing. You turn on a small clip-on light on one leg so pedestrians see it. You pre-focus, enable a 2-second self-timer, and step back so no one bumps you. Between gusts of wind and passerby lulls, you make three exposures; you review later and find one tack sharp because the timing aligned with a quiet moment.
subjects.reflection_question: When forced to compromise—center column up for height, or lower height but perfect rigidity—how do you decide which choice preserves the image you care about most, and how could you test that judgment before a once-in-a-lifetime shot?

subjects.subject: microeconomics
subjects.title: Utility and Indifference Curves
subjects.lesson:
First principles: preferences are about order, not measurement. Utility is a way to label which bundles of goods a person prefers, but the numbers themselves have no physical meaning. If coffee and bagels bundle A is preferred to bundle B, any utility function that assigns a higher number to A than to B works. Double, square, or log the numbers—so long as the ranking stays the same, choices are unchanged. That is ordinal utility.

Indifference curves: maps of equal satisfaction
An indifference curve is the set of all bundles that a person values equally. Imagine axes: coffee on one, bagels on the other. Each curve shows combinations that leave the person just as satisfied. Higher curves (farther from the origin) represent more preferred bundles, because they contain more of at least one good without giving up too much of the other.

Typical properties (when both goods are “goods”)
- Downward sloping: If you give up some coffee, you need more bagels to keep satisfaction unchanged.
- Do not cross: If two curves crossed, you’d have contradictory rankings.
- Convex to the origin: The curve bows inward, reflecting diminishing marginal rate of substitution. As you have more coffee and fewer bagels, you require larger bribes of bagels to give up another cup of coffee. Variety is valued.

Marginal rate of substitution (MRS)
The MRS at a point is the slope (in absolute value) of the indifference curve: the rate at which a person is willing to trade one good for the other while staying on the same curve. It captures willingness to substitute, not a cardinal measure of happiness. Because utility is ordinal, only this slope and the ordering of curves matter for choice.

Choices under a budget
Introduce a budget line: all bundles you can afford given prices and income. The chosen bundle is where a reachable indifference curve is highest. At an interior optimum, the budget line is tangent to an indifference curve, so MRS equals the price ratio (coffee to bagels). This is not about equating utilities across goods; it’s about trading at market prices until no further voluntary gains are possible—your willingness to trade equals the market’s required trade.

Revealed preference and ordinal utility
We don’t have to look inside the mind or measure happiness. Observed choices reveal which bundles are on higher curves. If a person picks A over B when both are affordable, A lies on a higher indifference curve. Over many choices, we can infer the shape of their preference map without ever assigning a meaningful numeric utility.

Non-standard shapes and what they mean
- Perfect substitutes: Straight, downward-sloping lines. The person is willing to trade goods at a constant rate (e.g., 1 can of cola for 1 can of a rival brand).
- Perfect complements: Right angles (L-shaped). Goods are only valuable in fixed proportions (e.g., left and right shoes).
- Satiation or “bliss point”: Curves circle around a best bundle; beyond that, more of a good can reduce satisfaction. Then “more is better” no longer holds.

Comparisons across people
Ordinal utility does not support interpersonal comparisons. Saying you get utility 10 and I get 8 from pizza says nothing objective about who enjoys it more. Policy questions about equity need other frameworks (like social welfare functions or fairness principles).

Income and substitution effects (intuition)
When a price changes, two forces move the choice: the substitution effect (relative prices changed, so the slope of the budget line changed) and the income effect (purchasing power changed). Indifference curves help separate these conceptually: slide along a curve for substitution; jump to a different curve for income.

Why this matters
- Predicting demand: Tangencies and reachable curves explain why quantity demanded slopes down (usually) as prices rise.
- Designing bundles: Businesses create product mixes that line up with typical indifference maps (e.g., offering sizes that match substitution patterns).
- Understanding heterogeneity: Different shapes imply different responses to price and income.

Limitations and care
- Indifference curves assume stable, complete, and transitive preferences. Real humans have context effects and framing biases.
- Ordinal utility can’t tell how strong a preference is, only which option ranks higher. For strength, we use choice under risk (expected utility) or experiments, but those add assumptions.

The core takeaway: Choices reveal an ordering of bundles. Indifference curves are a picture of that ordering. Budgets pick the highest attainable curve, and tangency conditions describe the exact trade-off at the margin—without ever measuring happiness itself.
subjects.example: You buy breakfast with $12. Coffee is $3, bagels are $3. The budget line runs from 4 coffees (0 bagels) to 4 bagels (0 coffees). You prefer variety; your indifference curves are convex. At equal prices, the tangency is near 2 coffees and 2 bagels. If coffee’s price rises to $4, the budget line rotates inward around 4 bagels. Your new tangency might be 1 coffee and 2.5 bagels, showing a substitution toward bagels and a drop in total satisfaction (a lower reachable curve). We never needed to assign numbers to your utility; the map and tangency told the story.
subjects.scenario: A mobile plan bundles data (GB) and streaming hours. You face a fixed budget and prices that convert dollars into the two services. Your indifference curves are steep at low data because you cannot function without maps and messages; you’d trade many streaming hours for 1GB early on. As data increases, your MRS falls—you’re willing to give up fewer streaming hours per extra GB. The plan you choose is where the budget line is tangent to a curve: if the provider discounts streaming heavily (changing the slope), you slide along your curves toward more streaming. The firm anticipates this by offering tiers that match typical MRS patterns.
subjects.reflection_question: If two bundles are chosen at different times under the same budget and prices, what must have changed in the person’s indifference map, and how could you detect whether it was a shift in tastes or an unnoticed change in constraints?

subjects.subject: Microsoft 365 Administrator (MS-102)
subjects.title: Custom domains and DNS configuration
subjects.lesson:
First principles: name resolution maps human-friendly names to services. For Microsoft 365, DNS records prove you own a domain and direct mail, Teams, and other traffic to Microsoft’s endpoints. Stability and security depend on accurate records and careful change timing.

Pre-checks and roles
- You need appropriate admin rights (Global Administrator or equivalent delegated permissions) in Microsoft 365/Entra ID.
- Know where your DNS is hosted (registrar or DNS provider). You will add or update records there. Microsoft does not host your DNS; it guides you to configure it.
- Plan TTLs. Shorten to 300–600 seconds a day before cutover for quick rollback; raise later for stability.

Add and verify a custom domain
1) In Microsoft 365 admin center, go to Settings > Domains > Add domain. Enter your domain (example.com).
2) Choose Verify. Microsoft provides a TXT record, typically “MS=ms########”.
3) At your DNS host, add a TXT record at the root (@) with that value. TTL 300–3600 seconds.
4) Wait for propagation, then click Verify. Once verified, the domain is added to your tenant.
5) Optionally set the domain as default for new users or aliases. Consider UPN suffix changes in Entra ID if you want user sign-ins as user@example.com.

Configure DNS for Exchange Online
Required core records:
- MX: Points inbound mail to Exchange Online. Format: example-com.mail.protection.outlook.com with priority (often 0 or 10). Keep only one MX for reliability unless directed.
- SPF (TXT): v=spf1 include:spf.protection.outlook.com -all. If you send mail from other services (e.g., marketing platform), add their includes before -all.
- Autodiscover (CNAME): autodiscover.example.com -> autodiscover.outlook.com. Enables clients to auto-configure.
- DKIM: Two CNAMEs, typically selector1._domainkey.example.com -> selector1-example-com._domainkey.<initial-domain>.onmicrosoft.com and selector2 similarly. Enable DKIM signing in Exchange admin center after records exist.
- DMARC (TXT): _dmarc.example.com with a policy, e.g., v=DMARC1; p=none; rua=mailto:dmarc@example.com; fo=1. Start with p=none to gather reports; move to quarantine/reject once alignment is confirmed.

Optional but helpful:
- MTA-STS: A TXT policy and a hosted policy file to improve SMTP TLS security, plus a TLS-RPT TXT for reports. Not required, but good hygiene.

Configure DNS for Teams
Teams uses SIP-related records historically associated with Skype for Business:
- CNAME: sip.example.com -> sipdir.online.lync.com
- CNAME: lyncdiscover.example.com -> webdir.online.lync.com
- SRV: _sip._tls.example.com -> sipdir.online.lync.com port 443 priority 100 weight 1
- SRV: _sipfederationtls._tcp.example.com -> sipfed.online.lync.com port 5061 priority 100 weight 1
These enable sign-in and federation. Some tenants work without all of these when using only the Teams desktop/web apps, but add them for full compatibility, especially with devices and federation.

Propagation and validation
- Use nslookup/dig to confirm records. For example: dig MX example.com, dig TXT example.com, dig CNAME autodiscover.example.com.
- In Microsoft 365 admin center Domains page, select the domain and run Troubleshoot or “Continue setup” to let Microsoft check for missing records.

Cutover planning for email
- Lower MX TTL a day early. On cutover, switch MX to Microsoft 365 and update SPF.
- Keep the old system receiving for a short overlap if possible, or set up forwarding. Monitor non-delivery reports (NDRs).
- After stable delivery, raise TTLs.

Common pitfalls and fixes
- Multiple MX records pointing to old and new systems can cause random delivery. Use one authoritative MX.
- SPF too long or multiple TXT SPF records: Combine into one record, keep under 255 chars per string (use quotes to split) and under the 10-DNS-lookup limit.
- DKIM not signing: Wait for DNS to propagate, then enable per domain in Exchange admin center.
- Autodiscover conflicts: Legacy on-prem records may need removal when fully cloud.

Security hygiene
- Lock down registrar access with MFA.
- Monitor DMARC reports for spoofing attempts.
- Periodically re-check DNS records after registrar changes.

Mental model: names route traffic. Verification proves control; MX and CNAMEs steer clients; TXT policies govern trust. A careful sequence—verify, publish, test, cut over—keeps mail flowing and calls connecting.
subjects.example: A small firm moves from a web host’s POP mail to Exchange Online. On Monday, the admin lowers MX TTL to 300s. Tuesday morning, she adds the Microsoft TXT verification record and verifies the domain. She publishes SPF (include:spf.protection.outlook.com), Autodiscover CNAME, and the Teams SRV/CNAME records. She sets up DKIM CNAMEs but waits to enable. At 7 p.m., she points MX to example-com.mail.protection.outlook.com and removes the old MX. She bulk assigns Exchange Online licenses, waits, and tests by sending mail in and out. She enables DKIM, sets DMARC to p=none, and watches reports for a week before raising the MX TTL back to 3600s.
subjects.scenario: An IT consultant inherits a tenant where users sign in with user@contoso.onmicrosoft.com and mail flows through a legacy gateway. She adds contoso.com, verifies it, and plans a staged cutover. She publishes new SPF that includes both the gateway and Microsoft 365, adds Autodiscover, and pre-creates mailboxes. On cutover night, she updates MX to Microsoft 365 and keeps the gateway as an outbound smart host for two days to catch any firewall rules she missed. After monitoring logs and DMARC reports with no issues, she removes the gateway from SPF, updates outbound connectors, and rotates DKIM keys.
subjects.reflection_question: Given equal effort, would you keep DNS at the current registrar with short TTLs for agility, or move to a central provider like Azure DNS for control and auditing, and how would that choice change your rollback and monitoring plan during email cutover?

subjects.subject: wisdom
subjects.title: First Principles Thinking
subjects.lesson:
First principles thinking means building understanding from bedrock facts instead of copying surface patterns. You ask: What is the problem really made of? Which constraints are laws, and which are customs? By separating assumptions from necessities, you discover levers that others miss.

The core loop
1) Clarify the goal. Define success in plain, measurable terms. If the statement is fuzzy, the solution will be too.
2) List constraints. Mark which are hard (physics, math, law) and which are soft (policy, habit, vendor preference). Hard constraints shape the sandbox; soft ones can be negotiated.
3) Decompose. Split the problem into components simple enough to reason about. Name inputs, outputs, and couplings. If a piece is murky, split it again.
4) Model simply. Use the lightest model that respects the hard constraints: back-of-the-envelope math, unit checks, orders of magnitude. Seek the gist before the details.
5) Test edges. What happens at extremes? If a design fails gracefully only in the middle, you have a fragility.
6) Recombine and iterate. Rebuild the solution from the parts. Cut anything not serving the defined goal. Then test against reality and refine.

Tools you can use
- Assumption hunting: Write every premise. Ask, “What evidence supports this? What would change my mind?”
- Fermi estimation: Rough numbers reveal feasibility. If a result is off by 10x, it saves months of effort.
- Base rates: Start with typical outcomes before layering story-specific details.
- Dimensional analysis: Check that units work out; it catches nonsense.
- Inversion: Ask how to guarantee failure; then avoid those conditions.

Examples of hard vs soft constraints
- Hard: A battery’s energy density; the speed of light; legal weight limits for trucks; a budget cap of $50,000 if it cannot be changed.
- Soft: “We always use vendor X”; “Marketing needs a dashboard on Tuesdays”; office seating charts.

Benefits
- Novel solutions: When you ignore tradition, you see options like eliminating steps or changing interfaces.
- Robust decisions: Grounding plans in constraints makes them resilient to opinion swings.
- Clear communication: You can explain the why behind each design choice.

Risks and guardrails
- Over-reduction: You can strip away context that matters (human factors, incentives). Keep a “reality loop” with users and stakeholders.
- Analysis paralysis: Infinite decomposition stalls action. Timebox the modeling and bias toward testing small prototypes.
- Charm of the clever: The simplest model that works is best; complexity often hides fragility.

A compact checklist
- Define the job-to-be-done.
- Separate hard from soft constraints.
- Break into parts and map interfaces.
- Create a minimal model; sanity-check with numbers.
- Probe edge cases; identify failure modes.
- Recombine; cut waste; test; iterate.

A mental picture
Imagine the problem as a machine of gears. You remove the casing (tradition), pull out each gear (subproblem), read its specs (constraints), then reassemble the machine with only the gears needed, in a cleaner configuration. If the output meets the goal with fewer gears and less friction, you’ve thought from first principles.

Communication from first principles
When presenting, lead with constraints and the reconstructed logic: “We need X under Y limits; given physics A and budget B, only designs C and D are feasible; we tested both; D wins on reliability.” This frames disagreement around facts and trade-offs, not preferences.

Cultivating the habit
- Practice on small daily problems. Ask “Why do we do it this way?” and try an alternative designed from constraints.
- Keep an assumptions log. Revisit it after a project to see which assumptions held and which failed.
- Pair the method with fast experiments. Reality checks keep the model honest.

The essence: Think like a builder of systems, not a follower of recipes. Start from what must be true, design what could be true, and only then adopt the parts of tradition that earn their keep.
subjects.example: Your team thinks it needs a custom analytics platform because “we have 10 million events per day.” You restate the goal: answer product questions within 2 hours at <$2,000/month, retaining 13 months of history. Hard constraints: event volume, latency, budget, privacy law. Soft constraints: “We prefer vendor X.” You decompose: ingestion, storage, query, dashboards. Fermi math shows raw compressed logs are ~200 GB/month; a columnar store with partitioning can handle it. You prototype with a managed data warehouse and object storage; queries return in minutes. You cut costly stream processors that don’t change outcomes. The team gets the answers on time, under budget, with a simpler stack.
subjects.scenario: A community clinic thinks longer hours will reduce patient wait times. You define the goal: 80% of patients seen within 20 minutes. Hard constraints: number of rooms, staffing certification rules, average visit time. Soft constraints: current appointment scheduler, habitual shift patterns. You model flow with a rough queue estimate: arrivals peak 8–10 a.m.; rooms idle mid-afternoon. Instead of adding hours, you redesign: staggered shifts to cover the morning peak, a fast-track nurse protocol for simple visits, and online pre-check forms to cut room time. A one-week pilot hits the 20-minute target without increasing total hours.
subjects.reflection_question: Which assumption in a current project, if proven false tomorrow, would most upend your plan, and how can you cheaply test that assumption this week to either invalidate it or bind it into a hard constraint you can design around?

subjects.subject: fiction-writing
subjects.title: Inciting Incident
subjects.lesson:
First principles: story begins when normal ends and choice begins. The inciting incident is the disruptive event that knocks the protagonist out of stasis and forces a new pursuit. It frames the core question of the book: What will they do now? Without this disruption, scenes may entertain, but there is no story engine.

What an inciting incident must do
- Break normalcy: It alters the protagonist’s world in a way that cannot be ignored or easily reversed.
- Demand agency: It creates a problem only the protagonist can or will address. Even if triggered by fate or another character, it becomes personal.
- Set stakes: It makes consequences visible and rising on the first rung of the Stakes Ladder. Loss, gain, identity, love, freedom—something that matters.
- Point to the story spine: It implies the goal, antagonist force, and arena. It promises the kind of struggle we’re about to read.

Timing and placement
Readers need enough of “normal” to feel the break, but not so much that they drift. A common calibration is within the first 10–15% of the story. Before it lands, seed the protagonist’s desire, flaw, and wound, so the incident hooks into them and forces growth. Place it onstage if possible. Offstage incidents can work if their impact explodes on page, but direct experience is more visceral.

Calibrating scale and momentum
Big doesn’t always mean effective. The right scale is what forces a decision today. A city exploding offscreen might not incite your introvert unless it touches their need. A denied promotion that threatens a custody case can push harder than a meteor. Increase momentum by compressing time and narrowing exits: deadlines, vanishing options, and irreversible steps.

Types of inciting incidents
- Call to adventure: A tempting opportunity or summons invites pursuit.
- Attack on normal: A threat destroys or corrupts what the protagonist values.
- Discovery: A revelation shifts understanding and forces a new goal.
- Betrayal: Trust breaks, recasting relationships and obligations.
- Ultimatum: An authority or loved one forces a painful choice.

Design from character outward
The incident should hit the fault line between desire and fear. If the hero wants freedom but fears abandonment, the incident should make freedom contingent on risking being left or leaving someone. Tie the disruption to a latent capability the character resists using; that tension creates internal conflict and agency.

Scene craftsmanship
- Entry: Establish the ordinary world in motion, not summary. A specific routine amplifies the sense of loss when broken.
- Impact moment: Show the break as action with sensory detail. Let the protagonist react in their unique voice.
- Decision path: Even if the full commitment (Point of No Return) comes later, the inciting incident should force immediate action or a short chain of actions.
- Aftershock: A beat where the cost and stakes are clarified. Plant a clock or closing door.

Common pitfalls
- Randomness without relevance: A car crash that doesn’t tie to the protagonist’s wants is noise, not incitement.
- Too soft: If the protagonist could reasonably walk away, readers might. Make inaction costlier than action.
- Too late: If readers don’t know what’s normal for the character, they won’t feel the break—but if you wait too long, patience runs out. Thread character quickly, then break.
- Info-dump: Don’t pause the shock to explain the world. Let questions pull readers forward.

Iteration trick
Write three different inciting incidents for the same premise: one external attack, one enticing opportunity, one devastating discovery. Then choose the one that best fuses your protagonist’s desire, wound, and the central conflict.

The essence: The inciting incident kicks the door open and points down the hallway. It doesn’t solve anything. It makes not moving impossible, and moving costly, in exactly the way that will forge the protagonist into who they must become.
subjects.example: Premise: A brilliant, risk-averse archivist wants a quiet life. Inciting incident options you draft: 1) Attack on normal: A fire destroys the archive; only she knows a hidden ledger that could expose the arsonists. 2) Opportunity: A stranger offers her a once-in-a-century chance to decode a newly found cipher, but the work requires travel with a team she distrusts. 3) Discovery: She finds a forged donor record implicating her mentor in art theft. You pick #3 because it collides with her desire for safety and her fear of betrayal. On the page, the discovery scene shows her noticing anachronistic ink under UV light, then the mentor walking in as she pockets the evidence. Stakes rise: if she stays quiet, the crimes continue; if she speaks, she loses her protector and risks her job.
subjects.scenario: You’re writing a contemporary YA novel. The protagonist, a scholarship debater supporting a sick parent, keeps her head down to avoid attention. The inciting incident: the school audits finances and revokes her scholarship over a clerical error, due in two weeks unless she pays $3,000. This disrupts normal (she’ll be forced to drop out), demands agency (only she can solve it), sets stakes (family stability, future), and points to the arena (the city’s underground debate circuit with cash prizes). The aftershock scene reveals that her main rival runs the circuit; to win, she must form a team with someone she swore never to trust.
subjects.reflection_question: What single, concrete change to your protagonist’s ordinary world would force them to make a decision today that they have successfully avoided for years, and how does that decision ignite the central conflict rather than merely complicate their schedule?
